error_code: str = 'QKD_OPTIMIZATION_ERROR'
qkdpy.wrap_exception(original: Exception, qkd_exception_class: type[QKDException] = <class 'qkdpy.exceptions.QKDException'>, message: str | None = None) → QKDException[source]

Wrap a standard exception in a QKD exception.

The wrapped exception carries the original via Python’s __cause__, so tracebacks preserve the full chain.

Parameters:

  • original – Original exception to wrap

  • qkd_exception_class – QKD exception class to use

  • message – Optional custom message (defaults to original message)

Returns:

Wrapped QKD exception with original as cause

class qkdpy.QiskitIntegration[source]

Integration with Qiskit quantum computing framework.

__init__() → None[source]

Initialize Qiskit integration.

qubit_to_qiskit(qkdpy_qubit: Qubit) → Statevector[source]

Convert a QKDpy Qubit to a Qiskit Statevector.

Parameters:

qkdpy_qubit – QKDpy Qubit object

Returns:

Qiskit Statevector representing the same quantum state

qiskit_to_qubit(qiskit_state: Statevector) → Qubit[source]

Convert a Qiskit Statevector to a QKDpy Qubit.

Parameters:

qiskit_state – Qiskit Statevector

Returns:

QKDpy Qubit representing the same quantum state

state_from_label(label: str) → Statevector[source]

Create a Qiskit Statevector from a standard basis label.

Supports labels like ‘0’, ‘1’, ‘+’, ‘-’, ‘r’, ‘l’ (single-qubit) and multi-qubit labels like ‘00’, ‘++’, ‘01’, etc.

Parameters:

label – Standard basis label

Returns:

Qiskit Statevector for the given label

compute_concurrence(state: DensityMatrix | Statevector) → float[source]

Compute the concurrence of a 2-qubit quantum state.

Concurrence ranges from 0 (separable) to 1 (maximally entangled).

Parameters:

state – 2-qubit DensityMatrix or Statevector

Returns:

Concurrence value

compute_entanglement_of_formation(state: DensityMatrix | Statevector) → float[source]

Compute the entanglement of formation of a 2-qubit state.

Parameters:

state – 2-qubit DensityMatrix or Statevector

Returns:

Entanglement of formation value

compute_negativity(state: DensityMatrix | Statevector) → float[source]

Compute the negativity of a quantum state.

Negativity measures entanglement based on the partial transpose.

Parameters:

state – DensityMatrix or Statevector (2-qubit for standard measure)

Returns:

Negativity value

compute_mutual_information(state: DensityMatrix | Statevector) → float[source]

Compute the mutual information of a bipartite state.

Mutual information I(A:B) = S(A) + S(B) - S(AB) measures total correlations between subsystems.

Parameters:

state – Bipartite DensityMatrix or Statevector

Returns:

Mutual information value

compute_partial_trace(state: DensityMatrix | Statevector, qargs: list[int]) → DensityMatrix[source]

Compute the partial trace over specified qubits.

Parameters:

  • state – DensityMatrix or Statevector

  • qargs – List of qubit indices to trace out

Returns:

Reduced DensityMatrix

compute_schmidt_decomposition(state: Statevector, qargs: list[int]) → list[tuple[float, Statevector, Statevector]][source]

Compute the Schmidt decomposition of a bipartite state.

Parameters:

  • state – Statevector

  • qargs – Two qubit indices defining the bipartition

Returns:

List of (coefficient, left_state, right_state) tuples

compute_von_neumann_entropy(state: DensityMatrix | Statevector) → float[source]

Compute the von Neumann entropy of a quantum state.

For a subsystem entropy, call compute_partial_trace() first to obtain the reduced density matrix.

Parameters:

state – DensityMatrix or Statevector

Returns:

von Neumann entropy value

compute_state_fidelity(state1: Statevector | DensityMatrix, state2: Statevector | DensityMatrix) → float[source]

Compute the fidelity between two quantum states.

Parameters:

  • state1 – First quantum state

  • state2 – Second quantum state

Returns:

Fidelity value (0 to 1)

to_density_matrix(state: Statevector) → DensityMatrix[source]

Convert a Statevector to a DensityMatrix.

Parameters:

state – Statevector to convert

Returns:

DensityMatrix representation

stabilizer_from_stabilizers(stabilizers: list[str]) → StabilizerState[source]

Create a StabilizerState from a list of Pauli stabilizer strings.

Useful for efficient simulation of large entanglement swapping networks.

Parameters:

stabilizers – List of Pauli strings (e.g., [‘XX’, ‘ZZ’])

Returns:

StabilizerState

create_bb84_circuit(num_qubits: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None) → QuantumCircuit[source]

Create a Qiskit circuit implementing the BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to use

  • alice_bases – List of bases Alice uses (‘Z’ for computational, ‘X’ for Hadamard)

  • bob_bases – List of bases Bob uses (‘Z’ for computational, ‘X’ for Hadamard)

Returns:

Qiskit QuantumCircuit implementing BB84

simulate_bb84_with_qiskit(num_qubits: int = 10, noise_model: str | None = None, noise_level: float = 0.0) → tuple[list[int], list[int], list[bool]][source]

Simulate BB84 protocol using Qiskit.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to simulate

  • noise_model – Type of noise to add (‘bit_flip’, ‘phase_flip’, ‘depolarizing’)

  • noise_level – Noise level (0.0 to 1.0)

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_bits, bob_bits, matching_bases)

convert_channel_to_qiskit(qkdpy_channel: QuantumChannel) → Any[source]

Convert a QKDpy QuantumChannel to a Qiskit NoiseModel.

Parameters:

qkdpy_channel – QKDpy QuantumChannel

Returns:

Qiskit NoiseModel representing the same channel characteristics

create_e91_circuit(num_pairs: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None) → QuantumCircuit[source]

Create a Qiskit circuit implementing the E91 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_pairs – Number of entangled pairs

  • alice_bases – List of bases Alice uses (‘Z’, ‘X’, ‘W’ for various angles)

  • bob_bases – List of bases Bob uses

Returns:

Qiskit QuantumCircuit implementing E91

simulate_e91_with_qiskit(num_pairs: int = 10, noise_model: str | None = None, noise_level: float = 0.0) → tuple[list[int], list[int], list[str], list[str]][source]

Simulate E91 protocol using Qiskit.

Parameters:

  • num_pairs – Number of pairs to simulate

  • noise_model – Type of noise

  • noise_level – Noise level

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_bits, bob_bits, alice_bases, bob_bases)

benchmark_qkdpy_vs_qiskit(num_qubits: int = 100, num_trials: int = 10) → dict[str, Any][source]

Benchmark QKDpy against Qiskit for BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to simulate

  • num_trials – Number of trials to run

Returns:

Dictionary with benchmark results

class qkdpy.CirqIntegration[source]

Integration with Cirq quantum computing framework.

__init__() → None[source]

Initialize Cirq integration.

qubit_to_cirq(qkdpy_qubit: Qubit) → Any[source]

Convert a QKDpy Qubit to a Cirq state.

Parameters:

qkdpy_qubit – QKDpy Qubit object

Returns:

Cirq-compatible state (raw numpy state vector in Cirq 1.x, which can be passed directly to cirq.Simulator().simulate(…, initial_state=…) instead of the removed StateVectorSimulationState(state_vector=…) constructor.

cirq_to_qubit(cirq_state: Any) → Qubit[source]

Convert a Cirq state to a QKDpy Qubit.

Parameters:

cirq_state – Cirq state

Returns:

QKDpy Qubit representing the same quantum state

create_bb84_circuit(num_qubits: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None) → Any[source]

Create a Cirq circuit implementing the BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to use

  • alice_bases – List of bases Alice uses (‘Z’ for computational, ‘X’ for Hadamard)

  • bob_bases – List of bases Bob uses (‘Z’ for computational, ‘X’ for Hadamard)

Returns:

Cirq Circuit implementing BB84

simulate_bb84_with_cirq(num_qubits: int = 10, noise_model: str | None = None, noise_level: float = 0.0) → tuple[list[int], list[int], list[bool]][source]

Simulate BB84 protocol using Cirq.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to simulate

  • noise_model – Type of noise to add (‘bit_flip’, ‘phase_flip’, ‘depolarizing’)

  • noise_level – Noise level (0.0 to 1.0)

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_bits, bob_bits, matching_bases)

convert_channel_to_cirq(qkdpy_channel: QuantumChannel) → list[Any][source]

Convert a QKDpy QuantumChannel to Cirq noise gates.

Parameters:

qkdpy_channel – QKDpy QuantumChannel

Returns:

List of Cirq gates representing the same channel characteristics

create_entanglement_circuit(num_pairs: int = 1) → Any[source]

Create a Cirq circuit for generating entangled pairs.

Parameters:

num_pairs – Number of entangled pairs to create

Returns:

Cirq Circuit creating entangled pairs

e91_with_cirq(num_pairs: int = 1) → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate the E91 protocol using Cirq entangled pairs.

Alice and Bob each measure their half of every Bell pair in a randomly chosen basis (Z or X), then sift the runs where their bases matched to build a shared raw key.

Parameters:

num_pairs – Number of entangled Bell pairs to generate.

Returns:

Dictionary with the sifted key and run metadata.

benchmark_qkdpy_vs_cirq(num_qubits: int = 100, num_trials: int = 10) → dict[str, Any][source]

Benchmark QKDpy against Cirq for BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to simulate

  • num_trials – Number of trials to run

Returns:

Dictionary with benchmark results

class qkdpy.PennyLaneIntegration[source]

Integration with PennyLane quantum computing framework.

__init__() → None[source]

Initialize PennyLane integration.

qubit_to_pennylane(qkdpy_qubit: Qubit) → Any[source]

Convert a QKDpy Qubit to a PennyLane state.

Parameters:

qkdpy_qubit – QKDpy Qubit object

Returns:

PennyLane tensor representing the same quantum state

pennylane_to_qubit(pennylane_state: Any) → Qubit[source]

Convert a PennyLane state to a QKDpy Qubit.

Parameters:

pennylane_state – PennyLane state

Returns:

QKDpy Qubit representing the same quantum state

compute_vn_entropy(state: ndarray, indices: list[int]) → float[source]

Compute the von Neumann entropy of a subsystem from a state.

Uses PennyLane’s math module for direct computation on state vectors or density matrices.

Parameters:

  • state – State vector or density matrix

  • indices – Subsystem indices to compute entropy for

Returns:

von Neumann entropy value

compute_mutual_info(state: ndarray, indices0: list[int], indices1: list[int]) → float[source]

Compute the mutual information between two subsystems.

Parameters:

  • state – State vector or density matrix

  • indices0 – First subsystem indices

  • indices1 – Second subsystem indices

Returns:

Mutual information value

compute_purity(state: ndarray, indices: list[int]) → float[source]

Compute the purity of a subsystem from a state.

Purity = Tr(ρ²) ranges from 1/d (maximally mixed) to 1 (pure).

Parameters:

  • state – State vector or density matrix

  • indices – Subsystem indices

Returns:

Purity value

compute_fidelity(state1: ndarray, state2: ndarray) → float[source]

Compute the fidelity between two quantum states.

Parameters:

  • state1 – First quantum state (state vector or density matrix)

  • state2 – Second quantum state

Returns:

Fidelity value (0 to 1)

compute_trace_distance(state1: ndarray, state2: ndarray) → float[source]

Compute the trace distance between two quantum states.

Parameters:

  • state1 – First quantum state

  • state2 – Second quantum state

Returns:

Trace distance value (0 to 1)

compute_chsh_correlation(alice_angles: list[float], bob_angles: list[float], num_qubits: int = 100) → float[source]

Compute the CHSH correlation value for DI-QKD verification.

The CHSH inequality |⟨S⟩| ≤ 2 is violated by quantum mechanics. A value > 2 indicates non-local correlations suitable for DI-QKD.

Uses exact analytic expectation values (shots parameter is accepted for backward compatibility but not used).

Parameters:

  • alice_angles – Measurement angles for Alice

  • bob_angles – Measurement angles for Bob

  • num_qubits – Ignored; kept for backward compatibility

Returns:

CHSH S-value

create_state_tomography_circuit(num_qubits: int = 1) → Any[source]

Create a circuit for quantum state tomography.

Measures in Z, X, and Y bases to reconstruct the density matrix.

Parameters:

num_qubits – Number of qubits to perform tomography on

Returns:

QNode that returns measurement results in all bases

create_e91_circuit(num_pairs: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None) → Any[source]

Create a PennyLane circuit implementing the E91 protocol.

Uses entangled pairs with measurement in Z, X, and W bases.

Parameters:

  • num_pairs – Number of entangled pairs

  • alice_bases – Bases Alice uses (‘Z’, ‘X’, ‘W’)

  • bob_bases – Bases Bob uses (‘Z’, ‘X’, ‘W’)

Returns:

QNode implementing E91

create_entanglement_circuit(num_pairs: int = 1) → Any[source]

Create a PennyLane circuit that generates Bell state entangled pairs.

Parameters:

num_pairs – Number of entangled pairs to create

Returns:

QNode that generates Bell states

create_bb84_circuit(num_qubits: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None) → Any[source]

Create a PennyLane circuit implementing the BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to use

  • alice_bases – List of bases Alice uses (‘Z’ for computational, ‘X’ for Hadamard)

  • bob_bases – List of bases Bob uses (‘Z’ for computational, ‘X’ for Hadamard)

Returns:

PennyLane quantum function implementing BB84

simulate_bb84_with_pennylane(num_qubits: int = 10, noise_model: str | None = None, noise_level: float = 0.0) → tuple[list[int], list[int], list[bool]][source]

Simulate BB84 protocol using PennyLane.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to simulate

  • noise_model – Type of noise to add (‘bit_flip’, ‘phase_flip’, ‘depolarizing’)

  • noise_level – Noise level (0.0 to 1.0)

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_bits, bob_bits, matching_bases)

convert_channel_to_pennylane(qkdpy_channel: QuantumChannel) → dict[str, Any][source]

Convert a QKDpy QuantumChannel to PennyLane noise parameters.

Parameters:

qkdpy_channel – QKDpy QuantumChannel

Returns:

Dictionary with PennyLane noise parameters

benchmark_qkdpy_vs_pennylane(num_qubits: int = 100, num_trials: int = 10) → dict[str, Any][source]

Benchmark QKDpy against PennyLane for BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to simulate

  • num_trials – Number of trials to run

Returns:

Dictionary with benchmark results

class qkdpy.QpiAIIntegration[source]

Integration with QpiAI Quantum SDK for QKD protocol simulation.

Provides conversion between QKDpy and QpiAI quantum primitives, circuit construction for QKD protocols, and access to QpiAI’s cloud quantum hardware and local simulators.

__init__() → None[source]

Initialize QpiAI integration.

qubit_to_qpiai(qkdpy_qubit: Qubit) → Statevector[source]

Convert a QKDpy Qubit to a QpiAI Statevector.

Parameters:

qkdpy_qubit – QKDpy Qubit object

Returns:

QpiAI Statevector representing the same quantum state

qpiai_to_qubit(qpiai_state: Statevector) → Qubit[source]

Convert a QpiAI Statevector to a QKDpy Qubit.

Parameters:

qpiai_state – QpiAI Statevector

Returns:

QKDpy Qubit representing the same quantum state

statevector_from_array(data: list[complex] | ndarray) → Statevector[source]

Create a QpiAI Statevector from an array of amplitudes (local, no cloud).

Parameters:

data – Complex amplitudes (will be normalized)

Returns:

QpiAI Statevector

compute_concurrence(state: ndarray) → float[source]

Compute the concurrence of a 2-qubit state.

Uses QpiAI’s formalism DensityMatrix.

Parameters:

state – 4x4 density matrix or 4-element state vector

Returns:

Concurrence value (0 to 1)

compute_purity(state: ndarray) → float[source]

Compute the purity of a quantum state.

Parameters:

state – Density matrix or state vector

Returns:

Purity value (1/d to 1)

create_bb84_circuit(num_qubits: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None, alice_bits: list[int] | None = None) → Circuit[source]

Create a QpiAI circuit implementing the BB84 protocol.

Alice and Bob operate on the same physical qubits (connected via quantum channel). Alice prepares, then Bob measures.

Parameters:

  • num_qubits – Number of qubits to encode

  • alice_bases – Bases Alice uses (‘Z’ or ‘X’)

  • bob_bases – Bases Bob uses (‘Z’ or ‘X’)

  • alice_bits – Alice’s bit values (0 or 1) for encoding

Returns:

QpiAI Circuit for BB84

create_entanglement_circuit(state_type: str = '|Ψ+>') → tuple[Circuit, str][source]

Create a circuit for Bell state (entangled pair) generation.

Parameters:

state_type – Type of Bell state: |Ψ+>, |Ψ->, |Φ+>, |Φ->

Returns:

Tuple of (QpiAI Circuit, description string)

create_ghz_circuit(num_qubits: int = 3) → Circuit[source]

Create a circuit for GHZ state generation.

GHZ state: (|0...0> + |1...1>)/sqrt(2) for multi-party QKD.

Parameters:

num_qubits – Number of qubits (>= 2)

Returns:

QpiAI Circuit for GHZ state

create_e91_circuit(num_pairs: int = 1, alice_bases: list[str] | None = None, bob_bases: list[str] | None = None) → Circuit[source]

Create a QpiAI circuit implementing the E91 protocol.

Uses entangled pairs with measurement in Z, X, and W bases.

Parameters:

  • num_pairs – Number of entangled pairs

  • alice_bases – Bases Alice uses (‘Z’, ‘X’, ‘W’)

  • bob_bases – Bases Bob uses (‘Z’, ‘X’, ‘W’)

Returns:

QpiAI Circuit for E91

simulate(circuit: Circuit, shots: int = 1024) → dict[str, Any][source]

Run a circuit on the QpiAI local simulator.

Requires API_KEY set in environment for cloud simulation. Falls back to returning the circuit object for inspection.

Parameters:

  • circuit – QpiAI Circuit to simulate

  • shots – Number of shots

Returns:

Dict with state information

submit_to_cloud(circuit: Circuit, device_name: str = 'QpiAI-QSV-Simulator', shots: int = 1024) → Any[source]

Submit a circuit to the QpiAI quantum cloud.

Parameters:

  • circuit – QpiAI Circuit to execute

  • device_name – Target device

  • shots – Number of measurement shots

Returns:

JobResult from the cloud execution

Raises:

ValueError – If API_KEY is not set

calculate_qber(alice_bits: list[int], bob_bits: list[int]) → float[source]

Calculate Quantum Bit Error Rate from measurement outcomes.

Parameters:

  • alice_bits – Alice’s bit string

  • bob_bits – Bob’s bit string

Returns:

QBER value (0 to 1)

compute_chsh_value(angle_settings: list[float]) → float[source]

Compute the CHSH S-value for DI-QKD verification.

Uses QpiAI Statevector to compute the Bell correlator.

Parameters:

angle_settings – List of four angles [a, a’, b, b’]

Returns:

CHSH S-value. S > 2 indicates non-local correlations.

class qkdpy.ErrorCorrection[source]

Provides various error correction methods for QKD protocols.

This class implements different error correction algorithms that can be used to reconcile Alice’s and Bob’s keys after the quantum transmission phase.

static cascade(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], block_sizes: list[int] | None = None, iterations: int = 4, random_permute: bool = True) → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Cascade error correction protocol with realistic improvements.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • block_sizes – List of block sizes for each iteration (default: [4, 8, 16, 32])

  • iterations – Number of iterations of the protocol

  • random_permute – Whether to randomly permute keys between iterations

Returns:

Tuple of corrected (alice_key, bob_key)

static winnow(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], block_size: int = 4, iterations: int = 4, random_permute: bool = True) → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Winnow error correction protocol with realistic improvements.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • block_size – Size of blocks for parity checks

  • iterations – Number of iterations of the protocol

  • random_permute – Whether to randomly permute keys between iterations

Returns:

Tuple of corrected (alice_key, bob_key)

static biconf(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], max_iterations: int = 10, error_rate_estimate: float = 0.1) → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

BICONF (Binary Confirmation) error correction protocol.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • max_iterations – Maximum number of iterations

  • error_rate_estimate – Estimated initial error rate

Returns:

Tuple of corrected (alice_key, bob_key)

static low_density_parity_check(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], code_rate: float = 0.5, max_iterations: int = 50) → tuple[list[int], list[int], int][source]

Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC) error correction with realistic implementation.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • code_rate – Code rate (information bits / total bits)

  • max_iterations – Maximum number of decoding iterations

Returns:

Tuple of corrected (alice_key, bob_key, number_of_iterations_used)

static ldpc(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], parity_check_matrix: ndarray | None = None, max_iterations: int = 100) → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

LDPC (Low-Density Parity-Check) error correction.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • parity_check_matrix – Parity check matrix for LDPC codes

  • max_iterations – Maximum number of iterations for the belief propagation algorithm

Returns:

Tuple of corrected (alice_key, bob_key)

static hamming_distance(key1: list[int], key2: list[int]) → int[source]

Calculate the Hamming distance between two keys.

Parameters:

  • key1 – First binary key

  • key2 – Second binary key

Returns:

Number of positions where the keys differ

static error_rate(key1: list[int], key2: list[int]) → float[source]

Calculate the error rate between two keys.

Parameters:

  • key1 – First binary key

  • key2 – Second binary key

Returns:

Fraction of positions where the keys differ

class qkdpy.AdvancedErrorCorrection[source]

Provides advanced error correction methods for QKD protocols.

static low_density_parity_check(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], rate: float = 0.5, max_iterations: int = 100) → tuple[list[int], list[int], bool][source]

Low-Density Parity-Check (LDPC) error correction.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • rate – Code rate (typically 0.5 for QKD)

  • max_iterations – Maximum number of belief propagation iterations

Returns:

Tuple of (corrected_alice_key, corrected_bob_key, success)

static polar_code_error_correction(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], noise_level: float = 0.1) → tuple[list[int], list[int], bool][source]

Polar code error correction for QKD.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • noise_level – Estimated noise level in the channel

Returns:

Tuple of (corrected_alice_key, corrected_bob_key, success)

static turbo_code_error_correction(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], max_iterations: int = 10) → tuple[list[int], list[int], bool][source]

Turbo code error correction for QKD.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • max_iterations – Maximum number of turbo decoding iterations

Returns:

Tuple of (corrected_alice_key, corrected_bob_key, success)

static fountain_code_error_correction(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], overhead: float = 0.1) → tuple[list[int], list[int], bool][source]

Fountain code error correction for QKD.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • overhead – Additional redundancy added to the key

Returns:

Tuple of (corrected_alice_key, corrected_bob_key, success)

static neural_network_error_correction(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], training_samples: int = 1000) → tuple[list[int], list[int], bool][source]

Neural network-based error correction for QKD.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s binary key

  • bob_key – Bob’s binary key

  • training_samples – Number of training samples to use

Returns:

Tuple of (corrected_alice_key, corrected_bob_key, success)

class qkdpy.PrivacyAmplification[source]

Provides various privacy amplification methods for QKD protocols.

This class implements different privacy amplification algorithms that can be used to reduce Eve’s information about the final key.

static universal_hashing(key: list[int], output_length: int, seed: int | None = None) → list[int][source]

Privacy amplification using universal hashing.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • seed – Seed for the random hash function

Returns:

Shortened, more secure key

static toeplitz_hashing(key: list[int], output_length: int, seed: int | None = None) → list[int][source]

Privacy amplification using Toeplitz matrix hashing.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • seed – Seed for the random hash function

Returns:

Shortened, more secure key

static cryptographic_hash(key: list[int], output_length: int, hash_algorithm: str = 'sha256') → list[int][source]

Privacy amplification using cryptographic hash functions.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • hash_algorithm – Hash algorithm to use (‘sha256’, ‘sha512’, etc.)

Returns:

Shortened, more secure key

static bennett_brassard_hashing(key: list[int], output_length: int, error_rate: float = 0.0) → list[int][source]

Privacy amplification using the Bennett-Brassard method.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • error_rate – Estimated error rate in the key

Returns:

Shortened, more secure key

static leftover_hash_lemma(key: list[int], min_entropy: float, security_parameter: float = 1e-09) → list[int][source]

Privacy amplification using the Leftover Hash Lemma.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • min_entropy – Minimum entropy of the input key

  • security_parameter – Desired security parameter

Returns:

Shortened, more secure key

static extract_randomness(key: list[int], output_length: int, method: str = 'universal_hashing') → list[int][source]

Extract randomness from a key using various methods.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to extract randomness from

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • method – Method to use for randomness extraction

Returns:

Random bits extracted from the key

class qkdpy.AdvancedPrivacyAmplification[source]

Provides advanced privacy amplification methods for QKD protocols.

static xor_extract(key: list[int], seed: int | None = None) → list[int][source]

Privacy amplification using XOR extraction with a seed.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • seed – Seed for the random extraction (optional)

Returns:

Extracted key with reduced length

Privacy amplification using AES-based hash extraction.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to be amplified

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

Returns:

Extracted key

Extract randomness from a key using various methods.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to extract randomness from

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • method – Method to use for randomness extraction (‘xor’, ‘aes’, ‘universal’)

Returns:

Random bits extracted from the key

static strong_extractor(key: list[int], output_length: int, min_entropy: float) → list[int][source]

Strong randomness extractor with formal security guarantees.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to extract randomness from

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • min_entropy – Minimum entropy of the input key

Returns:

Random bits extracted from the key

static seeded_extractor(key: list[int], seed: list[int], output_length: int) → list[int][source]

Seeded randomness extractor.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to extract randomness from

  • seed – Seed for the extractor

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

Returns:

Random bits extracted from the key

static multiple_independent_extractors(key: list[int], output_length: int, num_extractors: int = 3) → list[int][source]

Use multiple independent extractors and combine their outputs.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to extract randomness from

  • output_length – Desired length of the output key

  • num_extractors – Number of independent extractors to use

Returns:

Random bits extracted from the key

class qkdpy.KeyDistillation(error_correction_method: str = 'cascade', privacy_amplification_method: str = 'universal_hashing')[source]

Provides key distillation methods for QKD protocols.

This class combines error correction and privacy amplification to distill a secure key from the raw quantum transmission.

__init__(error_correction_method: str = 'cascade', privacy_amplification_method: str = 'universal_hashing') → None[source]

Initialize the key distillation process.

Parameters:

  • error_correction_method – Method for error correction

  • privacy_amplification_method – Method for privacy amplification

distill(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int], qber: float = 0.0, final_key_length: int | None = None) → dict[str, list[int] | float][source]

Perform key distillation.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s raw key

  • bob_key – Bob’s raw key

  • qber – Estimated Quantum Bit Error Rate

  • final_key_length – Desired length of the final key (optional)

Returns:

Dictionary containing distillation results and statistics

get_statistics() → dict[str, int | float][source]

Get distillation statistics.

Returns:

Dictionary containing distillation statistics

reset_statistics() → None[source]

Reset all statistics counters.

class qkdpy.QuantumKeyManager(channel: QuantumChannel)[source]

Manages quantum key generation, storage, and distribution.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel)[source]

Initialize the quantum key manager.

Parameters:

channel – Quantum channel for key generation

generate_key(session_id: str, key_length: int = 128, protocol: str = 'BB84') → str | None[source]

Generate a quantum key for a session.

Parameters:

  • session_id – Unique identifier for the session

  • key_length – Desired length of the key

  • protocol – QKD protocol to use

Returns:

Key identifier if successful, None otherwise

get_key(key_id: str) → list[int] | None[source]

Retrieve a key by its identifier.

Parameters:

key_id – Unique identifier for the key

Returns:

The key if found, None otherwise

delete_key(key_id: str) → bool[source]

Delete a key from storage.

Parameters:

key_id – Unique identifier for the key

Returns:

True if successful, False otherwise

get_session_keys(session_id: str) → list[str][source]

Get all key identifiers for a session.

Parameters:

session_id – Unique identifier for the session

Returns:

List of key identifiers

rotate_session_key(session_id: str, key_length: int = 128) → str | None[source]

Generate a new key for a session (key rotation).

Parameters:

  • session_id – Unique identifier for the session

  • key_length – Desired length of the new key

Returns:

New key identifier if successful, None otherwise

get_key_statistics() → dict[str, Any][source]

Get statistics about key generation and storage.

Returns:

Dictionary with key statistics

export_key_store(filename: str) → bool[source]

Export the key store to a file.

Parameters:

filename – Name of the file to export to

Returns:

True if successful, False otherwise

import_key_store(filename: str) → bool[source]

Import a key store from a file.

Parameters:

filename – Name of the file to import from

Returns:

True if successful, False otherwise

cleanup_expired_sessions(max_age: float = 3600.0) → int[source]

Remove expired sessions and their keys.

Parameters:

max_age – Maximum age of sessions in seconds

Returns:

Number of sessions cleaned up

class qkdpy.QuantumErrorCorrection[source]

Implementation of quantum error correction codes.

static shor_code_encode(qubit: Qubit) → list[Qubit][source]

Encode a single qubit using the 9-qubit Shor code.

The Shor code can correct arbitrary single-qubit errors.

Parameters:

qubit – The qubit to encode

Returns:

List of 9 qubits representing the encoded state

static shor_code_decode(qubits: list[Qubit]) → Qubit[source]

Decode a qubit from the 9-qubit Shor code.

Parameters:

qubits – List of 9 qubits encoded with Shor code

Returns:

Decoded qubit

static steane_code_encode(qubit: Qubit) → list[Qubit][source]

Encode a single qubit using the 7-qubit Steane code.

The Steane code is a [[7,1,3]] quantum error correction code that can correct arbitrary single-qubit errors.

Parameters:

qubit – The qubit to encode

Returns:

List of 7 qubits representing the encoded state

static steane_code_decode(qubits: list[Qubit]) → Qubit[source]

Decode a qubit from the 7-qubit Steane code.

Parameters:

qubits – List of 7 qubits encoded with Steane code

Returns:

Decoded qubit

static five_qubit_code_encode(qubit: Qubit) → list[Qubit][source]

Encode a single qubit using the 5-qubit perfect code.

The 5-qubit code is the smallest possible quantum error correction code that can correct arbitrary single-qubit errors.

Parameters:

qubit – The qubit to encode

Returns:

List of 5 qubits representing the encoded state

static five_qubit_code_decode(qubits: list[Qubit]) → Qubit[source]

Decode a qubit from the 5-qubit perfect code.

Parameters:

qubits – List of 5 qubits encoded with 5-qubit code

Returns:

Decoded qubit

static detect_and_correct_error(qubits: list[Qubit], error_type: str = 'X') → list[Qubit][source]

Detect and correct a single-qubit error using syndrome measurement.

Parameters:

  • qubits – List of qubits that may contain an error

  • error_type – Type of error to simulate (“X”, “Z”, or “Y”)

Returns:

Corrected qubits

static quantum_error_correction_simulation(initial_qubit: Qubit, code_type: str = 'shor', error_probability: float = 0.1) → tuple[Qubit, bool][source]

Simulate the complete quantum error correction process.

Parameters:

  • initial_qubit – The initial qubit to protect

  • code_type – Type of error correction code (“shor”, “steane”, “five_qubit”)

  • error_probability – Probability of error occurring on each qubit

Returns:

Tuple of (decoded_qubit, success) where success indicates if the decoded qubit matches the initial qubit

static get_code_parameters(code_type: str) → dict[source]

Get parameters for different quantum error correction codes.

Parameters:

code_type – Type of error correction code

Returns:

Dictionary with code parameters

static simulate_error_correction_performance(num_trials: int = 1000, code_type: str = 'shor', error_probability: float = 0.1) → dict[source]

Simulate the performance of quantum error correction.

Parameters:

  • num_trials – Number of trials to run

  • code_type – Type of error correction code

  • error_probability – Probability of error on each qubit

Returns:

Dictionary with performance statistics

class qkdpy.QKDOptimizer(protocol_name: str)[source]

Machine learning-based optimizer for QKD protocols.

__init__(protocol_name: str) → None[source]

Initialize the QKD optimizer.

Parameters:

protocol_name – Name of the QKD protocol to optimize

optimize_channel_parameters(parameter_space: dict[str, tuple[float, float]], objective_function: Callable[[dict[str, float]], float], num_iterations: int = 100, method: str = 'bayesian') → dict[str, Any][source]

Optimize quantum channel parameters using machine learning.

Parameters:

  • parameter_space – Dictionary mapping parameter names to (min, max) tuples

  • objective_function – Function to maximize (e.g., key rate, security)

  • num_iterations – Number of optimization iterations

  • method – Optimization method (‘bayesian’, ‘genetic’, ‘neural’, ‘gradient’)

Returns:

Dictionary with optimization results

get_optimization_history() → list[dict[str, Any]][source]

Get the history of all optimizations.

Returns:

List of optimization results

predict_performance(parameters: dict[str, float]) → float[source]

Predict protocol performance for given parameters using trained model.

Parameters:

parameters – Protocol parameters

Returns:

Predicted performance metric

class qkdpy.QKDAnomalyDetector[source]

Anomaly detection for QKD systems using machine learning.

__init__() → None[source]

Initialize the anomaly detector.

establish_baseline(metrics_history: list[dict[str, float]]) → None[source]

Establish baseline statistics from historical data.

Parameters:

metrics_history – List of historical metric dictionaries

detect_anomalies(current_metrics: dict[str, float]) → dict[str, bool][source]

Detect anomalies in current metrics.

current_metrics – Current metric values

Returns:

Dictionary mapping metric names to anomaly flags

update_anomaly_threshold(new_threshold: float) → None[source]

Update the anomaly detection threshold.

Parameters:

new_threshold – New threshold value (0.0 to 1.0)

get_detection_report() → dict[str, Any][source]

Generate a report of anomaly detection results.

Dictionary with detection statistics

class qkdpy.EfficientQKDPredictor(input_dim: int, *, max_memory_mb: int = 256, enable_quantization: bool = True, enable_pruning: bool = True, pruning_threshold: float = 0.01)[source]

Resource-efficient predictor for QKD parameter optimization.

Uses adaptive architecture that scales based on available resources, with support for model quantization and pruning.

__init__(input_dim: int, *, max_memory_mb: int = 256, enable_quantization: bool = True, enable_pruning: bool = True, pruning_threshold: float = 0.01) → None[source]

Initialize efficient predictor.

Parameters:

  • input_dim – Number of input features

  • max_memory_mb – Maximum memory budget in MB

  • enable_quantization – Whether to use INT8 quantization for inference

  • enable_pruning – Whether to prune small weights

  • pruning_threshold – Threshold for weight pruning

fit(X: ndarray, y: ndarray, *, epochs: int = 100, learning_rate: float = 0.01, batch_size: int | None = None, early_stopping_patience: int = 10, validation_split: float = 0.1) → dict[str, Any][source]

Train the model with early stopping.

Parameters:

  • X – Input features (n_samples, n_features)

  • y – Target values (n_samples,)

  • epochs – Maximum number of epochs

  • learning_rate – Learning rate

  • batch_size – Batch size (None for full batch)

  • early_stopping_patience – Epochs to wait before early stopping

  • validation_split – Fraction of data for validation

Returns:

Training history and statistics

predict(X: ndarray) → ndarray[source]

Make predictions.

Parameters:

X – Input features

Returns:

Predicted values

get_model_size_bytes() → int[source]

Get model size in bytes.

get_sparsity() → float[source]

Get fraction of zero weights (sparsity).

class qkdpy.KnowledgeDistillation(teacher_predict: Callable[[ndarray], ndarray], temperature: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.5)[source]

Knowledge distillation for transferring from large to small models.

Uses a teacher-student paradigm to train smaller, efficient models that approximate the behavior of larger, more accurate models.

__init__(teacher_predict: Callable[[ndarray], ndarray], temperature: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.5) → None[source]

Initialize knowledge distillation.

Parameters:

  • teacher_predict – Teacher model’s predict function

  • temperature – Softening temperature for soft targets

  • alpha – Weight for distillation loss vs hard label loss

generate_soft_targets(X: ndarray) → ndarray[source]

Generate soft targets from teacher model.

Parameters:

X – Input data

Returns:

Soft targets from teacher

distill(student: Any, X_train: ndarray, y_train: ndarray, **kwargs: Any) → dict[str, Any][source]

Train student model using knowledge distillation.

Parameters:

  • student – Student model to train (an EfficientQKDPredictor)

  • X_train – Training input data

  • y_train – Hard labels (ground truth)

  • **kwargs – Additional training arguments

Returns:

Training results

class qkdpy.AdaptiveModelSelector[source]

Automatically select appropriate model based on device capabilities.

static get_available_memory_mb() → int[source]

Estimate available memory in MB.

Returns:

Estimated available memory

static create_optimal_predictor(input_dim: int, *, use_quantization: bool | None = None) → EfficientQKDPredictor[source]

Create predictor with optimal settings for current device.

Parameters:

  • input_dim – Number of input features

  • use_quantization – Override quantization setting

Returns:

Optimally configured predictor

Protocol for channel condition predictors.

Satisfied by EfficientQKDPredictor and any other predictor implementing fit, predict, and is_trained.

property is_trained: bool

Whether the predictor has been trained.

fit(X: ndarray, y: ndarray, **kwargs: Any) → dict[str, Any][source]

Train the predictor on historical pass data.

Returns:

dict with at least epochs_trained and final_train_loss keys.

predict(X: ndarray) → ndarray[source]

Predict key yield for given atmospheric conditions.

__init__(*args, **kwargs)
class qkdpy.QuantumNetwork(name: str = 'Quantum Network', topology_type: str = 'custom')[source]

Represents a quantum network with multiple nodes and connections.

__init__(name: str = 'Quantum Network', topology_type: str = 'custom')[source]

Initialize a quantum network.

Parameters:

  • name – Name of the quantum network

  • topology_type – Type of network topology (‘custom’, ‘star’, ‘line’, ‘mesh’, ‘ring’)

add_node(node_id: str, protocol: BaseProtocol | None = None, position: tuple[float, float] | None = None) → None[source]

Add a node to the quantum network.

Parameters:

  • node_id – Unique identifier for the node

  • protocol – QKD protocol for the node (default: BB84)

  • position – Geographic position as (latitude, longitude) or (x, y) coordinates

add_connection(node1_id: str, node2_id: str, channel: QuantumChannel | None = None, distance: float | None = None, fiber_type: str = 'standard', has_repeater: bool = False) → None[source]

Add a quantum connection between two nodes.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

  • channel – Quantum channel for the connection (default: noiseless)

  • distance – Physical distance between nodes in km (if None, calculated from positions)

  • fiber_type – Type of optical fiber (‘standard’, ‘dispersion-shifted’, ‘hollow-core’)

  • has_repeater – Whether the connection has a quantum repeater

remove_node(node_id: str) → None[source]

Remove a node from the quantum network.

Parameters:

node_id – Identifier of the node to remove

get_shortest_path(source: str, destination: str, weight: str = 'distance') → list[str][source]

Find the shortest path between two nodes using Dijkstra’s algorithm.

Parameters:

  • source – Source node identifier

  • destination – Destination node identifier

  • weight – Weight attribute to use for path calculation (‘distance’, ‘latency’, or ‘loss’)

Returns:

List of node identifiers representing the shortest path

establish_key_between_nodes(node1_id: str, node2_id: str, key_length: int = 128, path_type: str = 'shortest', security_threshold: float = 0.11) → dict[str, Any] | None[source]

Establish a quantum key between two nodes in the network.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

  • key_length – Desired length of the key

  • path_type – Type of path to use (‘shortest’, ‘most_secure’, ‘lowest_loss’)

  • security_threshold – Maximum acceptable QBER for secure key

Returns:

Dictionary with key and metrics if successful, None otherwise

perform_entanglement_swapping(node1_id: str, node2_id: str) → bool[source]

Perform entanglement swapping between two nodes via a relay.

This method simulates the physical process of entanglement swapping: 1. Establish entanglement between Node1 and Relay. 2. Establish entanglement between Relay and Node2. 3. Perform Bell measurement at Relay. 4. Result is entanglement between Node1 and Node2.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

Returns:

True if successful (entanglement established), False otherwise

get_network_statistics() → dict[str, Any][source]

Get statistics about the quantum network.

Returns:

Dictionary with network statistics

simulate_network_performance(num_trials: int = 100, key_lengths: list[int] | None = None, path_selection: str = 'random') → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate the performance of the quantum network.

Parameters:

  • num_trials – Number of simulation trials

  • key_lengths – List of key lengths to test (default: [64, 128, 256])

  • path_selection – How to select paths (‘random’, ‘shortest’, ‘all_pairs’)

Returns:

Dictionary with simulation results

class qkdpy.MultiPartyQKD[source]

Multi-party quantum key distribution protocols.

static conference_key_agreement(network: QuantumNetwork, participants: list[str], key_length: int = 128) → dict[str, list[int]] | None[source]

Implement a conference key agreement protocol for multiple parties.

This is a simplified implementation that would use a hub-based approach in a real implementation.

Parameters:

  • network – Quantum network

  • participants – List of participant node identifiers

  • key_length – Desired length of the conference key

Returns:

Dictionary mapping participant IDs to their shares of the key, or None if the protocol fails

static quantum_secret_sharing(secret: list[int], num_shares: int, threshold: int) → list[list[int]][source]

Implement quantum secret sharing to distribute a secret.

This is a simplified classical implementation. A true quantum implementation would use quantum entanglement.

Parameters:

  • secret – Secret to share (list of bits)

  • num_shares – Number of shares to create

  • threshold – Minimum number of shares needed to reconstruct the secret

Returns:

List of secret shares

static reconstruct_secret(shares: list[list[int]]) → list[int][source]

Reconstruct a secret from its shares using XOR.

Parameters:

shares – List of secret shares

Returns:

Reconstructed secret

class qkdpy.SatelliteQKD(orbit_type: OrbitType = OrbitType.LEO, altitude_km: float = 500.0, ground_station_lat: float = 0.0, ground_station_lon: float = 0.0, protocol: str = 'BB84', channel_predictor: ChannelPredictor | None = None)[source]

Satellite-based Quantum Key Distribution system.

Simulates QKD between a satellite and ground station with: - Realistic orbital mechanics - Time-varying channel conditions - ML-based channel prediction for adaptive protocols

__init__(orbit_type: OrbitType = OrbitType.LEO, altitude_km: float = 500.0, ground_station_lat: float = 0.0, ground_station_lon: float = 0.0, protocol: str = 'BB84', channel_predictor: ChannelPredictor | None = None) → None[source]

Initialize satellite QKD system.

Parameters:

  • orbit_type – Type of satellite orbit

  • altitude_km – Satellite altitude in km

  • ground_station_lat – Ground station latitude

  • ground_station_lon – Ground station longitude

  • protocol – QKD protocol to use

  • channel_predictor – Optional ML predictor. Created lazily if None.

simulate_pass(duration_seconds: float = 300.0, time_steps: int = 60, atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile | None = None) → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate a satellite pass over the ground station.

Parameters:

  • duration_seconds – Pass duration in seconds

  • time_steps – Number of simulation time steps

  • atmosphere – Atmospheric conditions

Returns:

Pass simulation results

train_channel_predictor() → dict[str, Any][source]

Train ML model to predict channel conditions.

Uses historical pass data to train a predictor for optimal transmission windows.

Returns:

Training results

predict_key_yield(atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile, peak_elevation: float = 80.0) → float[source]

Predict key yield for given conditions using ML model.

Parameters:

  • atmosphere – Expected atmospheric conditions

  • peak_elevation – Expected peak elevation angle

Returns:

Predicted total key bits

get_mission_summary() → dict[str, Any][source]

Get summary of satellite QKD mission capabilities.

Returns:

Mission capability summary

class qkdpy.FreeSpaceOpticalChannel(satellite_position: SatellitePosition, atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile | None = None, wavelength_nm: float = 850.0, telescope_diameter_m: float = 0.3, pointing_error_urad: float = 1.0, **kwargs: Any)[source]

Quantum channel model for free-space optical links.

Implements realistic atmospheric effects including: - Geometric spreading loss - Atmospheric absorption and scattering - Turbulence-induced beam wandering and scintillation - Background noise from sky radiance

__init__(satellite_position: SatellitePosition, atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile | None = None, wavelength_nm: float = 850.0, telescope_diameter_m: float = 0.3, pointing_error_urad: float = 1.0, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Initialize free-space optical channel.

Parameters:

  • satellite_position – Satellite position data

  • atmosphere – Atmospheric profile

  • wavelength_nm – Wavelength of photons in nanometers

  • telescope_diameter_m – Receiving telescope diameter

  • pointing_error_urad – RMS pointing error in microradians

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments for base QuantumChannel

get_channel_metrics() → dict[str, float][source]

Get detailed channel metrics.

latitude: float

Satellite orbit types.

MEO = 'meo'
GEO = 'geo'

Quick satellite QKD simulation.

  • ground_lat – Ground station latitude

  • ground_lon – Ground station longitude

  • num_passes – Number of passes to simulate

Returns:

Simulation results

class qkdpy.BaseProtocol(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100)[source]

Abstract base class for QKD protocols.

This class defines the interface that all QKD protocol implementations should follow.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100)[source]

Initialize the protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

abstractmethod prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission.

Returns:

List of quantum states (qubits or qudits) to be sent through the channel

abstractmethod measure_states(states: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

Parameters:

states – List of received quantum states (may contain None for lost states)

Returns:

List of measurement results

abstractmethod sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys to keep only measurements in matching bases.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

abstractmethod estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER).

Returns:

Estimated QBER value

error_correction(alice_key: list[int], bob_key: list[int]) → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Perform error correction on the sifted keys.

Parameters:

  • alice_key – Alice’s sifted key

  • bob_key – Bob’s sifted key

Returns:

Tuple of corrected (alice_key, bob_key)

privacy_amplification(key: list[int], leak: int) → list[int][source]

Perform privacy amplification to reduce Eve’s information.

Parameters:

  • key – Key to be amplified

  • leak – Estimated amount of information leaked to Eve

Returns:

Shortened, more secure key

execute() → dict[str, list[int] | float | bool | dict[str, int | float | bool]][source]

Execute the full QKD protocol.

Returns:

Dictionary containing protocol results and statistics

reset() → None[source]

Reset the protocol state.

class qkdpy.BB84(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.11)[source]

Implementation of the BB84 quantum key distribution protocol.

BB84 is the first and most well-known quantum key distribution protocol, developed by Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard in 1984.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.11)[source]

Initialize the BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum QBER value considered secure

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission.

In BB84, Alice randomly chooses bits and bases, and prepares qubits in the corresponding states.

Returns:

List of qubits to be sent through the quantum channel

measure_states(states: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

In BB84, Bob randomly chooses bases to measure in.

Parameters:

states – List of received qubits (may contain None for lost qubits)

Returns:

List of measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys to keep only measurements in matching bases.

In BB84, Alice and Bob publicly compare their bases and keep only the bits where they used the same basis.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER).

In BB84, Alice and Bob publicly compare a subset of their sifted keys to estimate the error rate.

Returns:

Estimated QBER value

get_basis_reconciliation_rate() → float[source]

Calculate the basis reconciliation rate.

Returns:

Fraction of qubits where Alice and Bob used the same basis

get_key_rate() → float[source]

Calculate the key generation rate.

Returns:

Fraction of transmitted qubits that result in secure key bits

class qkdpy.DecoyStateBB84(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.11, weak_pulse_intensity: float = 0.1, decoy_intensity: float = 0.05)[source]

Implementation of the Decoy-State BB84 quantum key distribution protocol.

Decoy-State BB84 is an enhancement of the standard BB84 protocol that uses decoy states to detect photon number splitting (PNS) attacks and improve security against imperfect single-photon sources.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.11, weak_pulse_intensity: float = 0.1, decoy_intensity: float = 0.05)[source]

Initialize the Decoy-State BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum QBER value considered secure

  • weak_pulse_intensity – Intensity for weak coherent pulses (signal states)

  • decoy_intensity – Intensity for decoy states

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission with decoy states.

In Decoy-State BB84, Alice randomly chooses bits, bases, and intensities for each pulse, and prepares weak coherent pulses with the chosen parameters.

Returns:

List of qubits to be sent through the quantum channel

measure_states(qubits: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

In Decoy-State BB84, Bob randomly chooses bases to measure in.

Parameters:

qubits – List of received qubits (may contain None for lost qubits)

Returns:

List of measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys to keep only measurements in matching bases.

In Decoy-State BB84, Alice and Bob publicly compare their bases and keep only the bits where they used the same basis.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) using decoy state analysis.

In Decoy-State BB84, QBER is estimated separately for signal and decoy states.

Returns:

Estimated QBER value

analyze_decoy_states() → dict[source]

Analyze decoy state statistics for security parameter estimation.

Returns:

Dictionary with decoy state analysis results

calculate_secure_key_rate() → float[source]

Calculate the secure key generation rate using decoy state analysis.

Returns:

Secure key rate (bits per pulse)

get_basis_reconciliation_rate() → float[source]

Calculate the basis reconciliation rate.

Returns:

Fraction of pulses where Alice and Bob used the same basis

get_key_rate() → float[source]

Calculate the key generation rate.

Returns:

Fraction of transmitted pulses that result in secure key bits

class qkdpy.E91(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.1)[source]

Implementation of the E91 quantum key distribution protocol.

E91 is a QKD protocol proposed by Artur Ekert in 1991, based on quantum entanglement and Bell’s inequality. This implementation uses true entangled state simulation via MultiQubitState.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.1)[source]

Initialize the E91 protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum QBER value considered secure

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Any][source]

Prepare entangled quantum states.

In this simulation, we generate pairs on demand during measurement. Returns placeholders.

measure_states(states: Sequence[Qubit | Any | None]) → list[int][source]

Distribute and measure entangled states.

Parameters:

states – Placeholders

Returns:

List of Bob’s measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift keys for key generation.

We keep bits where Alice and Bob used the same measurement basis (angle). Matching pairs: - Alice pi/4 (idx 1) and Bob pi/4 (idx 0) - Alice pi/2 (idx 2) and Bob pi/2 (idx 1)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate QBER.

test_bell_inequality() → dict[str, Any][source]

Test CHSH inequality.

We use the non-matching bases for the CHSH test. Standard Ekert91 uses: Alice: 0 (A1), pi/4 (A2), pi/2 (A3) Bob: pi/4 (B1), pi/2 (B2), 3pi/4 (B3)

S = E(A1, B1) - E(A1, B3) + E(A3, B1) + E(A3, B3) Angles: A1=0, B1=pi/4 -> diff=pi/4 -> E ~ 0.707 A1=0, B3=3pi/4 -> diff=3pi/4 -> E ~ -0.707 A3=pi/2, B1=pi/4 -> diff=pi/4 -> E ~ 0.707 A3=pi/2, B3=3pi/4 -> diff=pi/4 -> E ~ 0.707

S = 0.707 - (-0.707) + 0.707 + 0.707 = 2.828

execute() → dict[source]

Execute the E91 protocol.

class qkdpy.SARG04(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100)[source]

Implementation of the SARG04 quantum key distribution protocol.

SARG04 is a variant of BB84 proposed by Scarani, Acín, Ribordy, and Gisin in 2004. It is more robust to certain types of eavesdropping attacks than BB84.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100) → None[source]

Initialize the SARG04 protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission.

In SARG04, Alice prepares states like BB84 but also selects a ‘partner’ state to form a non-orthogonal set for announcement.

Mapping: - Bit 0: |0> (Z) or |+> (X) - Bit 1: |1> (Z) or |-> (X)

Announcement Pairs (one sent, one partner with opposite bit): - If sending |0> (Bit 0), partner is |-> (Bit 1). Set: {|0>, |->} - If sending |1> (Bit 1), partner is |+> (Bit 0). Set: {|1>, |+>} - If sending |+> (Bit 0), partner is |1> (Bit 1). Set: {|+>, |1>} - If sending |-> (Bit 1), partner is |0> (Bit 0). Set: {|->, |0>}

Returns:

List of quantum states (qubits or qudits) to be sent through the quantum channel

measure_states(states: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

Bob measures in a random basis.

Parameters:

states – List of received quantum states (may contain None for lost states)

Returns:

List of measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys based on SARG04 logic.

Bob keeps the bit if his measurement result is orthogonal to one of the states in the announced set. This allows him to infer the other state was the one sent.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER).

get_sifting_efficiency() → float[source]
class qkdpy.B92(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.25)[source]

Implementation of the B92 quantum key distribution protocol.

B92 is a QKD protocol proposed by Charles Bennett in 1992. It uses only two non-orthogonal states instead of four as in BB84.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.25)[source]

Initialize the B92 protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum QBER value considered secure

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission.

In B92, Alice randomly chooses bits and prepares qubits in the corresponding non-orthogonal states.

Returns:

List of qubits to be sent through the quantum channel

measure_states(qubits: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

In B92, Bob measures in the Hadamard basis and interprets the results.

Parameters:

qubits – List of received qubits (may contain None for lost qubits)

Returns:

List of measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys to keep only conclusive measurements.

In B92, only certain measurement outcomes are kept for key generation.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER).

In B92, Alice and Bob publicly compare a subset of their sifted keys to estimate the error rate.

Returns:

Estimated QBER value

get_sifting_efficiency() → float[source]

Calculate the sifting efficiency of the protocol.

Returns:

Fraction of received qubits that are included in the sifted key

class qkdpy.CVQKD(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.1, modulation_variance: float = 4.0, homodyne_efficiency: float = 0.9, excess_noise: float = 0.01)[source]

Implementation of a continuous-variable QKD protocol (GG02).

This implementation simulates a Gaussian-modulated coherent state (GMCS) protocol using true continuous variables (quadratures) rather than discrete qubit approximations. It uses homodyne detection and reverse reconciliation.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.1, modulation_variance: float = 4.0, homodyne_efficiency: float = 0.9, excess_noise: float = 0.01)[source]

Initialize the CV-QKD protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum excess noise level considered secure

  • modulation_variance – Variance of Alice’s modulation (in shot noise units)

  • homodyne_efficiency – Efficiency of Bob’s homodyne detector

  • excess_noise – Excess noise in the channel (in shot noise units)

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Any][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission.

In this simulation, we generate the classical data that would be encoded into quantum states. The actual quantum transmission is simulated mathematically in the measurement step to allow for realistic continuous variable noise models that aren’t supported by the discrete Qubit class.

Returns:

List of None (placeholder, as we manage state internally)

measure_states(_: Sequence[Qubit | Any | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states using Homodyne detection.

Parameters:

states – Placeholder list

Returns:

List of measurement results (quantized for compatibility)

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the keys (Parameter Estimation phase).

In CV-QKD, sifting means Alice keeps the variable Bob measured.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_data, bob_data) as floats

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the error rate (or rather, excess noise/covariance).

For CV-QKD, we don’t use QBER directly but rather covariance and excess noise. However, to satisfy the BaseProtocol interface, we return a normalized error metric.

get_excess_noise() → float[source]

Calculate the actual excess noise from data.

execute() → dict[source]

Execute the CV-QKD protocol with post-processing.

class qkdpy.EnhancedCVQKD(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.1, modulation_variance: float = 2.0, detection_efficiency: float = 0.6)[source]

Enhanced implementation of continuous-variable QKD protocol.

This implementation properly handles continuous variables and implements Gaussian-modulated coherent state CV-QKD with homodyne detection.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.1, modulation_variance: float = 2.0, detection_efficiency: float = 0.6)[source]

Initialize the enhanced CV-QKD protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum excess noise level considered secure

  • modulation_variance – Variance of Gaussian modulation

  • detection_efficiency – Homodyne detection efficiency

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission.

In CV-QKD, Alice prepares coherent states with Gaussian modulation on both quadratures. For compatibility with the base protocol, we encode the continuous information in qubit states.

Alice’s bit value is encoded into the sign of her X-quadrature modulation so that Bob’s sign-based bit inference (sign of his X-quadrature measurement) is correlated with Alice’s bit. Without this, the sifted-key QBER is decorrelated and unphysical.

Returns:

List of qubits to be sent through the quantum channel

measure_states(qubits: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

In CV-QKD, Bob performs homodyne measurements on the received signals. For compatibility with the base protocol, we return discrete measurements.

Parameters:

qubits – List of received qubits (may contain None for lost signals)

Returns:

List of measurement results (0 or 1)

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys using correlated data.

In CV-QKD, key sifting is based on correlation between Alice’s modulations and Bob’s measurements.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the quantum bit error rate.

In CV-QKD, this is estimated from the correlation between Alice’s modulations and Bob’s measurements.

Returns:

Estimated error rate

calculate_secret_fraction() → float[source]

Calculate the fraction of secret key that can be extracted.

This uses the Gaussian optimality of Gaussian attacks.

Returns:

Secret fraction (key rate)

get_excess_noise() → float[source]

Estimate the excess noise in the channel.

Returns:

Estimated excess noise level

get_key_rate() → float[source]

Calculate the key generation rate.

Returns:

Key rate (bits per signal)

get_protocol_parameters() → dict[source]

Get the protocol parameters.

Returns:

Dictionary with protocol parameters

class qkdpy.DeviceIndependentQKD(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 2.0)[source]

Implementation of a device-independent QKD protocol (based on CHSH).

This implementation simulates a true entanglement-based protocol using MultiQubitState to represent Bell pairs. It verifies security through the violation of the CHSH inequality.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 2.0)[source]

Initialize the device-independent QKD protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Minimum Bell inequality violation for security

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Any][source]

Prepare quantum states.

In this entanglement-based protocol, we generate pairs on demand during the ‘measure’ phase to simulate the source distributing them. We return placeholders to satisfy the interface.

measure_states(states: Sequence[Qubit | Any | None]) → list[int][source]

Distribute and measure entangled states.

Parameters:

states – Placeholders

Returns:

List of Bob’s measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift keys for key generation.

We only use results where both Alice and Bob chose setting 0 (Key Generation).

test_bell_inequality() → dict[str, float][source]

Calculate CHSH statistic S using settings 1 and 2.

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate QBER.

execute() → dict[source]

Execute the full QKD protocol.

Returns:

Dictionary containing protocol results and statistics

class qkdpy.TwistedPairQKD(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.11)[source]

Twisted Pair QKD protocol implementation.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, security_threshold: float = 0.11) → None[source]

Initialize the Twisted Pair QKD protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qubit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • security_threshold – Maximum QBER value considered secure

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission with twisted encoding.

Returns:

List of qubits to be sent through the quantum channel

measure_states(states: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states with twisted decoding.

Parameters:

states – List of received quantum states (may contain None for lost states)

Returns:

List of measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys to keep only measurements in matching bases.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER).

Returns:

Estimated QBER value

get_twist_efficiency() → float[source]

Calculate the efficiency of the twisting mechanism.

Returns:

Fraction of qubits that were twisted

get_basis_distribution() → dict[str, dict[int | str, int] | int][source]

Analyze the distribution of measurement bases.

Returns:

Dictionary with basis distribution statistics

class qkdpy.HDQKD(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, dimension: int = 4, security_threshold: float = 0.15)[source]

Implementation of High-Dimensional Quantum Key Distribution (HD-QKD).

Utilizes qudits (d-level quantum systems) to encode information.

NOTE: This implementation currently supports full Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) construction only for PRIME dimensions (d = 2, 3, 5, 7, …). For non-prime dimensions, it falls back to a computational/Fourier basis pair which may not provide optimal security guarantees.

__init__(channel: QuantumChannel, key_length: int = 100, dimension: int = 4, security_threshold: float = 0.15) → None[source]

Initialize the HD-QKD protocol.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel for qudit transmission

  • key_length – Desired length of the final key

  • dimension – Dimension of the qudit system (d)

  • security_threshold – Maximum QBER value considered secure

prepare_states() → list[Qubit | Qudit][source]

Prepare quantum states for transmission in HD-QKD.

In HD-QKD, Alice randomly chooses symbols from {0, 1, …, d-1} and bases.

Returns:

List of qudits encoded with HD information

measure_states(qudits: Sequence[Qubit | Qudit | None]) → list[int][source]

Measure received quantum states.

In HD-QKD, Bob randomly chooses bases from the MUBs to measure in.

Parameters:

qudits – List of received qudits (may contain None for lost qudits)

Returns:

List of measurement results

sift_keys() → tuple[list[int], list[int]][source]

Sift the raw keys to keep only measurements in matching bases.

Returns:

Tuple of (alice_sifted_key, bob_sifted_key)

estimate_qber() → float[source]

Estimate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) for HD-QKD.

Returns:

Estimated QBER value

get_dimension_efficiency() → float[source]

Calculate the efficiency gain from using higher dimensions.

Returns:

Efficiency factor compared to qubit-based protocols

get_basis_distribution() → dict[str, dict[int | str, int] | int][source]

Analyze the distribution of measurement bases.

Returns:

Dictionary with basis distribution statistics

static plot_quantum_channel_characteristics(channel: QuantumChannel, title: str = 'Quantum Channel Characteristics', figsize: tuple[int, int] = (12, 8)) → Figure[source]

Plot characteristics of a quantum channel.

Parameters:

  • channel – Quantum channel to visualize

  • title – Title for the plot

  • figsize – Figure size (width, height)

Matplotlib figure object

Visualization tools for protocol execution and performance.

Matplotlib figure object

class qkdpy.QuantumSimulator[source]

Advanced quantum system simulator for QKD analysis.

__init__() → None[source]

Initialize the quantum simulator.

simulate_channel_performance(channel: QuantumChannel, num_trials: int = 1000, initial_state: Qubit | None = None) → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate the performance of a quantum channel.

Parameters:

  • num_trials – Number of simulation trials

  • initial_state – Initial qubit state (default: |0>)

Returns:

Dictionary with simulation results

analyze_protocol_security(protocol: BaseProtocol, num_simulations: int = 100, eavesdropping_probability: float = 0.5) → dict[str, Any][source]

Analyze the security of a QKD protocol under eavesdropping.

Parameters:

  • protocol – QKD protocol to analyze

  • num_simulations – Number of security analysis simulations

  • eavesdropping_probability – Probability of eavesdropping

Returns:

Dictionary with security analysis results

benchmark_protocols(protocols: list[BaseProtocol], num_trials: int = 50) → dict[str, Any][source]

Benchmark multiple QKD protocols.

Parameters:

  • num_trials – Number of trials for each protocol

Returns:

Dictionary with benchmark results

get_simulation_history() → list[dict][source]

Get the history of all simulations.

Returns:

List of simulation records

clear_simulation_history() → None[source]

Clear the simulation history.

get_performance_statistics() → dict[str, Any][source]

Get overall performance statistics.

Returns:

Dictionary with performance statistics

Analyzer for quantum networks and multi-party QKD.

Dictionary with network analysis results

Dictionary with network performance results
