Dictionary with detection statistics

class qkdpy.ml.EfficientQKDPredictor(input_dim: int, *, max_memory_mb: int = 256, enable_quantization: bool = True, enable_pruning: bool = True, pruning_threshold: float = 0.01)[source]

Resource-efficient predictor for QKD parameter optimization.

Uses adaptive architecture that scales based on available resources, with support for model quantization and pruning.

__init__(input_dim: int, *, max_memory_mb: int = 256, enable_quantization: bool = True, enable_pruning: bool = True, pruning_threshold: float = 0.01) → None[source]

Initialize efficient predictor.

Parameters:

  • input_dim – Number of input features

  • max_memory_mb – Maximum memory budget in MB

  • enable_quantization – Whether to use INT8 quantization for inference

  • enable_pruning – Whether to prune small weights

  • pruning_threshold – Threshold for weight pruning

fit(X: ndarray, y: ndarray, *, epochs: int = 100, learning_rate: float = 0.01, batch_size: int | None = None, early_stopping_patience: int = 10, validation_split: float = 0.1) → dict[str, Any][source]

Train the model with early stopping.

Parameters:

  • X – Input features (n_samples, n_features)

  • y – Target values (n_samples,)

  • epochs – Maximum number of epochs

  • learning_rate – Learning rate

  • batch_size – Batch size (None for full batch)

  • early_stopping_patience – Epochs to wait before early stopping

  • validation_split – Fraction of data for validation

Returns:

Training history and statistics

predict(X: ndarray) → ndarray[source]

Make predictions.

Parameters:

X – Input features

Returns:

Predicted values

get_model_size_bytes() → int[source]

Get model size in bytes.

get_sparsity() → float[source]

Get fraction of zero weights (sparsity).

class qkdpy.ml.KnowledgeDistillation(teacher_predict: Callable[[ndarray], ndarray], temperature: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.5)[source]

Knowledge distillation for transferring from large to small models.

Uses a teacher-student paradigm to train smaller, efficient models that approximate the behavior of larger, more accurate models.

__init__(teacher_predict: Callable[[ndarray], ndarray], temperature: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.5) → None[source]

Initialize knowledge distillation.

Parameters:

  • teacher_predict – Teacher model’s predict function

  • temperature – Softening temperature for soft targets

  • alpha – Weight for distillation loss vs hard label loss

generate_soft_targets(X: ndarray) → ndarray[source]

Generate soft targets from teacher model.

Parameters:

X – Input data

Returns:

Soft targets from teacher

distill(student: Any, X_train: ndarray, y_train: ndarray, **kwargs: Any) → dict[str, Any][source]

Train student model using knowledge distillation.

Parameters:

  • student – Student model to train (an EfficientQKDPredictor)

  • X_train – Training input data

  • y_train – Hard labels (ground truth)

  • **kwargs – Additional training arguments

Returns:

Training results

class qkdpy.ml.AdaptiveModelSelector[source]

Automatically select appropriate model based on device capabilities.

static get_available_memory_mb() → int[source]

Estimate available memory in MB.

Returns:

Estimated available memory

Create predictor with optimal settings for current device.

Parameters:

  • input_dim – Number of input features

  • use_quantization – Override quantization setting

Returns:

Optimally configured predictor

Efficient neural network models for resource-constrained QKD deployment.

This module provides lightweight, resource-aware neural network architectures that can run efficiently on devices with limited memory and compute resources.

class qkdpy.ml.efficient_models.EfficientQKDPredictor(input_dim: int, *, max_memory_mb: int = 256, enable_quantization: bool = True, enable_pruning: bool = True, pruning_threshold: float = 0.01)[source]

  • max_memory_mb – Maximum memory budget in MB

Network Module

Network simulation for QKD.

class qkdpy.network.ChannelPredictor(*args, **kwargs)[source]

Protocol for channel condition predictors.

Satisfied by EfficientQKDPredictor and any other predictor implementing fit, predict, and is_trained.

property is_trained: bool

Whether the predictor has been trained.

fit(X: ndarray, y: ndarray, **kwargs: Any) → dict[str, Any][source]

Train the predictor on historical pass data.

Returns:

dict with at least epochs_trained and final_train_loss keys.

predict(X: ndarray) → ndarray[source]

Predict key yield for given atmospheric conditions.

__init__(*args, **kwargs)
class qkdpy.network.QuantumNetwork(name: str = 'Quantum Network', topology_type: str = 'custom')[source]

Represents a quantum network with multiple nodes and connections.

__init__(name: str = 'Quantum Network', topology_type: str = 'custom')[source]

Initialize a quantum network.

Parameters:

  • name – Name of the quantum network

  • topology_type – Type of network topology (‘custom’, ‘star’, ‘line’, ‘mesh’, ‘ring’)

add_node(node_id: str, protocol: BaseProtocol | None = None, position: tuple[float, float] | None = None) → None[source]

Add a node to the quantum network.

Parameters:

  • node_id – Unique identifier for the node

  • protocol – QKD protocol for the node (default: BB84)

  • position – Geographic position as (latitude, longitude) or (x, y) coordinates

add_connection(node1_id: str, node2_id: str, channel: QuantumChannel | None = None, distance: float | None = None, fiber_type: str = 'standard', has_repeater: bool = False) → None[source]

Add a quantum connection between two nodes.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

  • channel – Quantum channel for the connection (default: noiseless)

  • distance – Physical distance between nodes in km (if None, calculated from positions)

  • fiber_type – Type of optical fiber (‘standard’, ‘dispersion-shifted’, ‘hollow-core’)

  • has_repeater – Whether the connection has a quantum repeater

remove_node(node_id: str) → None[source]

Remove a node from the quantum network.

Parameters:

node_id – Identifier of the node to remove

get_shortest_path(source: str, destination: str, weight: str = 'distance') → list[str][source]

Find the shortest path between two nodes using Dijkstra’s algorithm.

Parameters:

  • source – Source node identifier

  • destination – Destination node identifier

  • weight – Weight attribute to use for path calculation (‘distance’, ‘latency’, or ‘loss’)

Returns:

List of node identifiers representing the shortest path

establish_key_between_nodes(node1_id: str, node2_id: str, key_length: int = 128, path_type: str = 'shortest', security_threshold: float = 0.11) → dict[str, Any] | None[source]

Establish a quantum key between two nodes in the network.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

  • key_length – Desired length of the key

  • path_type – Type of path to use (‘shortest’, ‘most_secure’, ‘lowest_loss’)

  • security_threshold – Maximum acceptable QBER for secure key

Returns:

Dictionary with key and metrics if successful, None otherwise

perform_entanglement_swapping(node1_id: str, node2_id: str) → bool[source]

Perform entanglement swapping between two nodes via a relay.

This method simulates the physical process of entanglement swapping: 1. Establish entanglement between Node1 and Relay. 2. Establish entanglement between Relay and Node2. 3. Perform Bell measurement at Relay. 4. Result is entanglement between Node1 and Node2.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

Returns:

True if successful (entanglement established), False otherwise

get_network_statistics() → dict[str, Any][source]

Get statistics about the quantum network.

Returns:

Dictionary with network statistics

simulate_network_performance(num_trials: int = 100, key_lengths: list[int] | None = None, path_selection: str = 'random') → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate the performance of the quantum network.

Parameters:

  • num_trials – Number of simulation trials

  • key_lengths – List of key lengths to test (default: [64, 128, 256])

  • path_selection – How to select paths (‘random’, ‘shortest’, ‘all_pairs’)

Returns:

Dictionary with simulation results

Represents a node in a quantum network.

Parameters:

List of neighbor node identifiers

Returns:

Shared key if it exists, None otherwise

Remove a shared key with a partner node.

Parameters:

partner_id – Identifier of the partner node

class qkdpy.network.MultiPartyQKD[source]

Multi-party quantum key distribution protocols.

static conference_key_agreement(network: QuantumNetwork, participants: list[str], key_length: int = 128) → dict[str, list[int]] | None[source]

Implement a conference key agreement protocol for multiple parties.

This is a simplified implementation that would use a hub-based approach in a real implementation.

Parameters:

  • network – Quantum network

  • participants – List of participant node identifiers

  • key_length – Desired length of the conference key

Returns:

Dictionary mapping participant IDs to their shares of the key, or None if the protocol fails

static quantum_secret_sharing(secret: list[int], num_shares: int, threshold: int) → list[list[int]][source]

Implement quantum secret sharing to distribute a secret.

This is a simplified classical implementation. A true quantum implementation would use quantum entanglement.

Parameters:

  • secret – Secret to share (list of bits)

  • num_shares – Number of shares to create

  • threshold – Minimum number of shares needed to reconstruct the secret

Returns:

List of secret shares

static reconstruct_secret(shares: list[list[int]]) → list[int][source]

Reconstruct a secret from its shares using XOR.

Parameters:

shares – List of secret shares

Returns:

Reconstructed secret

class qkdpy.network.MultiPartyQKDNetwork(nodes: list[str])[source]

Simulation of a multi-party QKD network.

nodes – List of node identifiers in the network

Parameters:

  • node2 – Second node identifier

  • channel – Quantum channel connecting the nodes

Returns:

True if channel was added successfully, False otherwise

Establish a pairwise key between two nodes.

Parameters:

  • node1 – First node identifier

  • node2 – Second node identifier

  • key_length – Desired key length

Returns:

Generated key if successful, None otherwise

Broadcast a key from source node to all other nodes.

Parameters:

  • key_length – Desired key length

Returns:

Dictionary mapping node identifiers to keys

Get network statistics.

Returns:

Dictionary with network statistics

Get the security event log.

Returns:

List of security events

simulate_network_attack(attack_type: str, target_nodes: list[str]) → dict[source]

Simulate a network attack.

Parameters:

  • attack_type – Type of attack (‘eavesdropping’, ‘man_in_the_middle’, ‘denial_of_service’)

  • target_nodes – Nodes targeted by the attack

Returns:

Dictionary with attack simulation results

generate_network_topology_graph() → dict[source]

Generate a representation of the network topology.

Returns:

Dictionary with nodes and edges for graph visualization

class qkdpy.network.SatelliteQKD(orbit_type: OrbitType = OrbitType.LEO, altitude_km: float = 500.0, ground_station_lat: float = 0.0, ground_station_lon: float = 0.0, protocol: str = 'BB84', channel_predictor: ChannelPredictor | None = None)[source]

Satellite-based Quantum Key Distribution system.

Simulates QKD between a satellite and ground station with: - Realistic orbital mechanics - Time-varying channel conditions - ML-based channel prediction for adaptive protocols

__init__(orbit_type: OrbitType = OrbitType.LEO, altitude_km: float = 500.0, ground_station_lat: float = 0.0, ground_station_lon: float = 0.0, protocol: str = 'BB84', channel_predictor: ChannelPredictor | None = None) → None[source]

Initialize satellite QKD system.

Parameters:

  • orbit_type – Type of satellite orbit

  • altitude_km – Satellite altitude in km

  • ground_station_lat – Ground station latitude

  • ground_station_lon – Ground station longitude

  • protocol – QKD protocol to use

  • channel_predictor – Optional ML predictor. Created lazily if None.

simulate_pass(duration_seconds: float = 300.0, time_steps: int = 60, atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile | None = None) → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate a satellite pass over the ground station.

Parameters:

  • duration_seconds – Pass duration in seconds

  • time_steps – Number of simulation time steps

  • atmosphere – Atmospheric conditions

Returns:

Pass simulation results

Train ML model to predict channel conditions.

Uses historical pass data to train a predictor for optimal transmission windows.

Returns:

Training results

Predict key yield for given conditions using ML model.

Parameters:

  • atmosphere – Expected atmospheric conditions

  • peak_elevation – Expected peak elevation angle

Returns:

Predicted total key bits

Get summary of satellite QKD mission capabilities.

Returns:

Mission capability summary

class qkdpy.network.FreeSpaceOpticalChannel(satellite_position: SatellitePosition, atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile | None = None, wavelength_nm: float = 850.0, telescope_diameter_m: float = 0.3, pointing_error_urad: float = 1.0, **kwargs: Any)[source]

Quantum channel model for free-space optical links.

Implements realistic atmospheric effects including: - Geometric spreading loss - Atmospheric absorption and scattering - Turbulence-induced beam wandering and scintillation - Background noise from sky radiance

__init__(satellite_position: SatellitePosition, atmosphere: AtmosphericProfile | None = None, wavelength_nm: float = 850.0, telescope_diameter_m: float = 0.3, pointing_error_urad: float = 1.0, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Initialize free-space optical channel.

Parameters:

  • satellite_position – Satellite position data

  • atmosphere – Atmospheric profile

  • wavelength_nm – Wavelength of photons in nanometers

  • telescope_diameter_m – Receiving telescope diameter

  • pointing_error_urad – RMS pointing error in microradians

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments for base QuantumChannel

get_channel_metrics() → dict[str, float][source]

Get detailed channel metrics.

Satellite orbit types.

LEO = 'leo'
MEO = 'meo'
GEO = 'geo'
qkdpy.network.simulate_satellite_qkd(altitude_km: float = 500.0, ground_lat: float = 28.5, ground_lon: float = -80.6, num_passes: int = 5) → dict[str, Any][source]

Quick satellite QKD simulation.

Parameters:

  • altitude_km – Satellite altitude

  • ground_lat – Ground station latitude

  • ground_lon – Ground station longitude

  • num_passes – Number of passes to simulate

Returns:

Simulation results

Satellite Quantum Key Distribution (SatQKD) Module.

This module provides simulation capabilities for satellite-based quantum key distribution, implementing realistic free-space optical channel models, atmospheric effects, and orbital mechanics for space-ground quantum links.

This module demonstrates the intersection of: - Space Technology: Orbital mechanics, satellite-ground links - Quantum Computing: QKD protocols adapted for space - AI/ML: Predictive channel modeling and adaptive protocols

class qkdpy.network.satellite_qkd.OrbitType(value)[source]

Satellite orbit types.

LEO = 'leo'
MEO = 'meo'
GEO = 'geo'

Atmospheric conditions affecting transmission.

CLEAR = 'clear'
HAZE = 'haze'
THIN_CLOUDS = 'thin_clouds'
TURBULENT = 'turbulent'

Advanced quantum network simulation for multi-party QKD.

class qkdpy.network.quantum_network.NodeView(graph: SimpleGraph)[source]

A view of graph nodes that supports both dict-style access and callable iteration.

Mirrors the networkx NodeView API so SimpleGraph and nx.Graph are interchangeable.

__init__(graph: SimpleGraph) → None[source]
class qkdpy.network.quantum_network.SimpleGraph[source]

Simple graph implementation if networkx is not available.

__init__() → None[source]
add_node(node: Any, **attrs: Any) → None[source]
add_edge(node1: Any, node2: Any, **attrs: Any) → None[source]
property nodes: NodeView
edges() → list[tuple[Any, Any]][source]
neighbors(node: Any) → set[Any][source]

Remove a node and all its edges from the graph.

Simple implementation of Dijkstra’s algorithm.

class qkdpy.network.quantum_network.QuantumNetwork(name: str = 'Quantum Network', topology_type: str = 'custom')[source]

Represents a quantum network with multiple nodes and connections.

__init__(name: str = 'Quantum Network', topology_type: str = 'custom')[source]

Initialize a quantum network.

Parameters:

  • name – Name of the quantum network

  • topology_type – Type of network topology (‘custom’, ‘star’, ‘line’, ‘mesh’, ‘ring’)

add_node(node_id: str, protocol: BaseProtocol | None = None, position: tuple[float, float] | None = None) → None[source]

Add a node to the quantum network.

Parameters:

  • node_id – Unique identifier for the node

  • protocol – QKD protocol for the node (default: BB84)

  • position – Geographic position as (latitude, longitude) or (x, y) coordinates

add_connection(node1_id: str, node2_id: str, channel: QuantumChannel | None = None, distance: float | None = None, fiber_type: str = 'standard', has_repeater: bool = False) → None[source]

Add a quantum connection between two nodes.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

  • channel – Quantum channel for the connection (default: noiseless)

  • distance – Physical distance between nodes in km (if None, calculated from positions)

  • fiber_type – Type of optical fiber (‘standard’, ‘dispersion-shifted’, ‘hollow-core’)

  • has_repeater – Whether the connection has a quantum repeater

remove_node(node_id: str) → None[source]

Remove a node from the quantum network.

Parameters:

node_id – Identifier of the node to remove

get_shortest_path(source: str, destination: str, weight: str = 'distance') → list[str][source]

Find the shortest path between two nodes using Dijkstra’s algorithm.

Parameters:

  • source – Source node identifier

  • destination – Destination node identifier

  • weight – Weight attribute to use for path calculation (‘distance’, ‘latency’, or ‘loss’)

Returns:

List of node identifiers representing the shortest path

establish_key_between_nodes(node1_id: str, node2_id: str, key_length: int = 128, path_type: str = 'shortest', security_threshold: float = 0.11) → dict[str, Any] | None[source]

Establish a quantum key between two nodes in the network.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

  • key_length – Desired length of the key

  • path_type – Type of path to use (‘shortest’, ‘most_secure’, ‘lowest_loss’)

  • security_threshold – Maximum acceptable QBER for secure key

Returns:

Dictionary with key and metrics if successful, None otherwise

perform_entanglement_swapping(node1_id: str, node2_id: str) → bool[source]

Perform entanglement swapping between two nodes via a relay.

This method simulates the physical process of entanglement swapping: 1. Establish entanglement between Node1 and Relay. 2. Establish entanglement between Relay and Node2. 3. Perform Bell measurement at Relay. 4. Result is entanglement between Node1 and Node2.

Parameters:

  • node1_id – Identifier of the first node

  • node2_id – Identifier of the second node

Returns:

True if successful (entanglement established), False otherwise

get_network_statistics() → dict[str, Any][source]

Get statistics about the quantum network.

Returns:

Dictionary with network statistics

simulate_network_performance(num_trials: int = 100, key_lengths: list[int] | None = None, path_selection: str = 'random') → dict[str, Any][source]

Simulate the performance of the quantum network.

Parameters:

  • num_trials – Number of simulation trials

  • key_lengths – List of key lengths to test (default: [64, 128, 256])

  • path_selection – How to select paths (‘random’, ‘shortest’, ‘all_pairs’)

Returns:

Dictionary with simulation results

class qkdpy.network.quantum_network.QuantumNode(node_id: str, protocol: BaseProtocol)[source]

Represents a node in a quantum network.

Parameters:

Add a neighbor to this node.

Parameters:

Remove a neighbor from this node.

neighbor_id – Identifier of the neighbor node to remove

store_key(partner_id: str, key: list[int]) → None[source]

Store a shared key with a partner node.

Parameters:

  • key – Shared key to store

get_key(partner_id: str) → list[int] | None[source]

Retrieve a shared key with a partner node.

partner_id – Identifier of the partner node

Returns:

Shared key if it exists, None otherwise

remove_key(partner_id: str) → None[source]

Remove a shared key with a partner node.

Parameters:

partner_id – Identifier of the partner node

class qkdpy.network.quantum_network.MultiPartyQKD[source]

Multi-party quantum key distribution protocols.

static conference_key_agreement(network: QuantumNetwork, participants: list[str], key_length: int = 128) → dict[str, list[int]] | None[source]

Implement a conference key agreement protocol for multiple parties.

This is a simplified implementation that would use a hub-based approach in a real implementation.

Parameters:

  • network – Quantum network

  • participants – List of participant node identifiers

  • key_length – Desired length of the conference key

Returns:

Dictionary mapping participant IDs to their shares of the key, or None if the protocol fails

static quantum_secret_sharing(secret: list[int], num_shares: int, threshold: int) → list[list[int]][source]

Implement quantum secret sharing to distribute a secret.

This is a simplified classical implementation. A true quantum implementation would use quantum entanglement.

Parameters:

  • secret – Secret to share (list of bits)

  • num_shares – Number of shares to create

  • threshold – Minimum number of shares needed to reconstruct the secret

Returns:

List of secret shares

static reconstruct_secret(shares: list[list[int]]) → list[int][source]

Reconstruct a secret from its shares using XOR.

Parameters:

shares – List of secret shares

Returns:

Reconstructed secret

Utils Module

Utility functions and visualization tools for QKDpy.

class qkdpy.utils.ProtocolVisualizer[source]

Visualization tools for QKD protocols.

Visualize the BB84 protocol.

Parameters:

  • alice_bits – Alice’s random bits

  • alice_bases – Alice’s random bases

  • bob_bases – Bob’s random bases

  • bob_results – Bob’s measurement results

Matplotlib figure object

Returns:

Matplotlib figure object

static plot_sarg04_protocol(alice_bits: list[int], alice_bases: list[str], bob_bases: list[str], bob_results: list[int], bob_guesses: list[int], title: str = 'SARG04 Protocol Visualization') → Figure[source]

Parameters:

  • alice_bits – Alice’s random bits

  • alice_bases – Alice’s random bases

  • bob_bases – Bob’s random bases

  • bob_results – Bob’s measurement results

  • bob_guesses – Bob’s guesses about Alice’s states

Returns:

Matplotlib figure object

class qkdpy.utils.ProtocolExecutionVisualizer[source]

Visualization tools for protocol execution and performance.

static plot_protocol_execution_timeline(protocol: BaseProtocol, title: str = 'Protocol Execution Timeline', figsize: tuple[int, int] = (12, 8)) → Figure[source]

Plot timeline of protocol execution steps.

Parameters:

  • protocol – Protocol to visualize

  • title – Title for the plot

  • figsize – Figure size (width, height)

Returns:

Matplotlib figure object

static plot_key_generation_performance(key_lengths: list[int], execution_times: list[float], qber_values: list[float], title: str = 'Key Generation Performance', figsize: tuple[int, int] = (12, 8)) → Figure[source]

Plot key generation performance metrics.

Parameters:

  • key_lengths – List of key lengths

  • execution_times – List of execution times

  • qber_values – List of QBER values

  • title – Title for the plot

  • figsize – Figure size (width, height)

Returns:

Matplotlib figure object

static plot_security_analysis(qber_values: list[float], secure_threshold: float, title: str = 'Security Analysis', figsize: tuple[int, int] = (10, 6)) → Figure[source]

Plot security analysis based on QBER values.

Parameters:

  • qber_values – List of QBER values

  • secure_threshold – Security threshold

  • title – Title for the plot

  • figsize – Figure size (width, height)

Returns:

Matplotlib figure object

static plot_protocol_comparison(protocol_results: dict[str, dict[str, Any]], metrics: list[str] | None = None, title: str = 'Protocol Comparison', figsize: tuple[int, int] = (12, 8)) → Figure[source]

Compare different protocols based on performance metrics.

Parameters:

  • protocol_results – Dictionary with protocol results

  • metrics – List of metrics to compare

  • title – Title for the plot

  • figsize – Figure size (width, height)

Returns:

Matplotlib figure object

  • initial_state – Initial qubit state (default: |0>)

Dictionary with simulation results

length – Length of the binary string

List of random bits

Returns:

Bytes represented by the bits

Convert bytes to a list of bits.

data – Bytes to convert

Returns:

List of bits representing the bytes

qkdpy.utils.bits_to_int(bits: list[int]) → int[source]

Convert a list of bits to an integer.

Parameters:

bits – List of bits to convert

Returns:

Integer represented by the bits

qkdpy.utils.int_to_bits(n: int, length: int | None = None) → list[int][source]

Convert an integer to a list of bits.

Parameters:

  • n – Integer to convert

  • length – Desired length of the bit list (optional)

Returns:

List of bits representing the integer

qkdpy.utils.hamming_distance(bits1: list[int], bits2: list[int]) → int[source]

Calculate the Hamming distance between two bit strings.

Parameters:

  • bits1 – First bit string

  • bits2 – Second bit string

Returns:

Number of positions where the bit strings differ

Raises:

ValueError – If the bit strings have different lengths

qkdpy.utils.binary_entropy(p: float) → float[source]

Calculate the binary entropy function.

Parameters:

p – Probability (between 0 and 1)

Returns:

Binary entropy value

qkdpy.utils.calculate_qber(alice_bits: list[int], bob_bits: list[int]) → float[source]

Calculate the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) between two bit strings.

Parameters:

  • alice_bits – Alice’s bit string

  • bob_bits – Bob’s bit string

Returns:

QBER value

Raises:

ValueError – If the bit strings have different lengths

qkdpy.utils.mutual_information(x: list[int], y: list[int]) → float[source]

Calculate the mutual information between two random variables.

Parameters:

  • y – Second random variable (list of integers)

Returns:

Mutual information value

Raises:

ValueError – If the lists have different lengths

qkdpy.utils.generate_random_permutation(n: int) → list[int][source]

Generate a random permutation of integers from 0 to n-1.

n – Length of the permutation

Returns:

Random permutation of integers

qkdpy.utils.apply_permutation(bits: list[int], permutation: list[int]) → list[int][source]

Apply a permutation to a list of bits.

Parameters:

  • permutation – Permutation to apply

Returns:

Permuted list of bits

Raises:

ValueError – If the permutation is not valid for the bit list

class qkdpy.utils.QKDLogger(name: str)[source]

Structured logger wrapper for QKDpy.

Each instance is a lightweight wrapper around a stdlib+structlog logger obtained via structlog.get_logger(name). The global structlog configuration is set at module-import time and never mutated by individual instances.

__init__(name: str) → None[source]

Initialize QKD logger.

Parameters:

name – Logger name (usually module name)

bind(**kwargs: Any) → QKDLogger[source]

Bind context data to logger.

unbind(*keys: str) → QKDLogger[source]

Remove context data from logger.

debug(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log debug message.

info(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log info message.

warning(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log warning message.

error(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log error message.

critical(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log critical message.

security(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log security-relevant event.

audit(action: str, *, actor: str | None = None, resource: str | None = None, result: str = 'success', **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log audit trail event.

qkdpy.utils.get_logger(name: str | None = None) → QKDLogger[source]

Get a QKD logger instance.

Does NOT reconfigure structlog globally — that happened once at module import.

Parameters:

name – Logger name (defaults to calling module)

Returns:

Configured QKDLogger instance wrapping structlog (or stdlib as fallback)

qkdpy.utils.configure_default_logger(level: str = 'INFO', json_output: bool = False) → QKDLogger[source]

Configure the default module-level logger.

Parameters:

  • level – Minimum log level (stdlib fallback only; structlog uses global config)

  • json_output – Whether to output JSON format (stdlib fallback only)

Returns:

Configured default logger

qkdpy.utils.log_debug(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log debug message using default logger.

qkdpy.utils.log_info(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log info message using default logger.

qkdpy.utils.log_warning(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log warning message using default logger.

qkdpy.utils.log_error(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log error message using default logger.

qkdpy.utils.log_security(event: str, **kwargs: Any) → None[source]

Log security event using default logger.

class qkdpy.utils.OperationSpan(operation: str, *, level: str = 'info', logger_override: Any = None, **context: Any)[source]

Context manager for timing and logging an operation.

Usage:

with OperationSpan("protocol.execute", protocol="BB84", key_length=256) as span:
    result = protocol.execute()
    span.set_metadata(qber=result["qber"], key_size=len(result["final_key"]))

Emits structured operation_started and operation_completed / operation_failed events with duration in milliseconds.

__init__(operation: str, *, level: str = 'info', logger_override: Any = None, **context: Any) → None[source]

Start an operation span.

Parameters:

  • operation – Stable operation name (e.g. protocol.execute)

  • level – Log level for completion events

  • logger_override – Logger instance to use (defaults to module logger)

  • **context – Static context attached to all events in this span

set_metadata(**metadata: Any) → None[source]

Attach additional metadata to the completion event.

qkdpy.utils.instrument(operation: str | None = None, *, level: str = 'info', log_args: bool = True, log_result: bool = False) → Callable[[F], F][source]

Decorator that instruments a function with an OperationSpan.

Usage:

@instrument("qkd.bb84.execute")
def execute(self, ...):
    ...

When operation is None, the fully-qualified function name module.function is used as the span name.

Parameters:

  • operation – Stable operation name (defaults to module.function)

  • level – Log level for the completion event

  • log_args – If True, attach call arguments to the started event

  • log_result – If True, attach the return value to the completed event

qkdpy.utils.record_protocol_execution(protocol_name: str, key_length: int, qber: float, final_key_size: int, is_secure: bool, duration_ms: float, channel_stats: dict[str, Any] | None = None) → None[source]

Emit a structured event for a completed protocol execution.

This is a convenience helper for the emit-on-completion pattern, separate from the per-step OperationSpan instrumentation.

Parameters:

  • protocol_name – Name of the protocol (e.g. BB84)

  • key_length – Requested key length in bits

  • qber – Measured quantum bit error rate

  • final_key_size – Size of the final distilled key

  • is_secure – Whether the protocol met the security threshold

  • duration_ms – Total execution time in milliseconds

  • channel_stats – Optional channel statistics dict

qkdpy.utils.record_ml_training(model_name: str, protocol: str, input_dim: int, training_samples: int, training_time_ms: float, final_loss: float | None = None, convergence_iterations: int | None = None) → None[source]

Emit a structured event for ML model training.

Parameters:

  • model_name – Name of the ML model

  • protocol – QKD protocol the model targets

  • input_dim – Input feature dimensionality

  • training_samples – Number of training samples used

  • training_time_ms – Training duration in milliseconds

  • final_loss – Final training loss value

  • convergence_iterations – Iterations to convergence

qkdpy.utils.record_qber_diagnostic(protocol: str, qber: float, threshold: float, key_size: int, distance_km: float | None = None) → None[source]

Emit a structured QBER diagnostic event.

Parameters:

  • protocol – Protocol name

  • qber – Measured QBER

  • threshold – Security threshold

  • key_size – Sifted key size

  • distance_km – Simulated channel distance in km

qkdpy.utils.validate_range(param_name: str, min_value: float | None = None, max_value: float | None = None, *, min_inclusive: bool = True, max_inclusive: bool = True) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate numeric parameter is within range.

Parameters:

  • param_name – Name of parameter to validate

  • min_value – Minimum allowed value (None for no minimum)

  • max_value – Maximum allowed value (None for no maximum)

  • min_inclusive – Whether minimum is inclusive

  • max_inclusive – Whether maximum is inclusive

Returns:

Decorator function

qkdpy.utils.validate_type(param_name: str, expected_types: type | tuple[type, ...], *, allow_none: bool = False) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate parameter type.

Parameters:

  • param_name – Name of parameter to validate

  • expected_types – Expected type or tuple of types

  • allow_none – Whether None is allowed

Returns:

Decorator function

qkdpy.utils.validate_positive(param_name: str, *, allow_zero: bool = False) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate parameter is positive.

Parameters:

  • param_name – Name of parameter to validate

  • allow_zero – Whether zero is allowed

Returns:

Decorator function

qkdpy.utils.validate_probability(param_name: str) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate parameter is a valid probability [0, 1].

Parameters:

param_name – Name of parameter to validate

Returns:

Decorator function

qkdpy.utils.validate_not_empty(param_name: str) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate sequence parameter is not empty.

Parameters:

param_name – Name of parameter to validate

Returns:

Decorator function

qkdpy.utils.validate_key_length(key: Sequence[int], min_length: int = 1) → None[source]

Validate key has sufficient length.

Parameters:

  • key – Binary key to validate

  • min_length – Minimum required length

Raises:

ParameterError – If key is too short

qkdpy.utils.validate_binary_key(key: Sequence[int]) → None[source]

Validate key contains only binary values (0 or 1).

Parameters:

key – Key to validate

Raises:

ParameterError – If key contains non-binary values

qkdpy.utils.validate_qber(qber: float) → None[source]

Validate QBER value is in valid range [0, 0.5].

Parameters:

qber – QBER value to validate

Raises:

RangeError – If QBER is out of valid range

qkdpy.utils.validate_unitary(matrix: ndarray, atol: float = 1e-10) → None[source]

Validate matrix is unitary.

Parameters:

  • matrix – Matrix to validate

  • atol – Absolute tolerance for comparison

Raises:

ParameterError – If matrix is not unitary

qkdpy.utils.validate_normalized_state(state: ndarray, atol: float = 1e-10) → None[source]

Validate quantum state is normalized.

Parameters:

  • state – State vector to validate

  • atol – Absolute tolerance for comparison

Raises:

ParameterError – If state is not normalized

qkdpy.utils.validate_density_matrix(rho: ndarray, atol: float = 1e-10) → None[source]

Validate density matrix properties.

Parameters:

  • rho – Density matrix to validate

  • atol – Absolute tolerance for comparison

Raises:

ParameterError – If matrix is not a valid density matrix

Instrumentation and telemetry for QKDpy operations.

Provides decorators and context managers for observing protocol execution, ML training, and other key operations with structured events and timing.

class qkdpy.utils.instrumentation.OperationSpan(operation: str, *, level: str = 'info', logger_override: Any = None, **context: Any)[source]

Context manager for timing and logging an operation.

Usage:

with OperationSpan("protocol.execute", protocol="BB84", key_length=256) as span:
    result = protocol.execute()
    span.set_metadata(qber=result["qber"], key_size=len(result["final_key"]))

Emits structured operation_started and operation_completed / operation_failed events with duration in milliseconds.

__init__(operation: str, *, level: str = 'info', logger_override: Any = None, **context: Any) → None[source]

Start an operation span.

Parameters:

  • operation – Stable operation name (e.g. protocol.execute)

  • level – Log level for completion events

  • logger_override – Logger instance to use (defaults to module logger)

  • **context – Static context attached to all events in this span

set_metadata(**metadata: Any) → None[source]

Attach additional metadata to the completion event.

qkdpy.utils.instrumentation.instrument(operation: str | None = None, *, level: str = 'info', log_args: bool = True, log_result: bool = False) → Callable[[F], F][source]

Decorator that instruments a function with an OperationSpan.

Usage:

@instrument("qkd.bb84.execute")
def execute(self, ...):
    ...

When operation is None, the fully-qualified function name module.function is used as the span name.

Parameters:

  • operation – Stable operation name (defaults to module.function)

  • level – Log level for the completion event

  • log_args – If True, attach call arguments to the started event

  • log_result – If True, attach the return value to the completed event

qkdpy.utils.instrumentation.record_protocol_execution(protocol_name: str, key_length: int, qber: float, final_key_size: int, is_secure: bool, duration_ms: float, channel_stats: dict[str, Any] | None = None) → None[source]

Emit a structured event for a completed protocol execution.

This is a convenience helper for the emit-on-completion pattern, separate from the per-step OperationSpan instrumentation.

Parameters:

  • protocol_name – Name of the protocol (e.g. BB84)

  • key_length – Requested key length in bits

  • qber – Measured quantum bit error rate

  • final_key_size – Size of the final distilled key

  • is_secure – Whether the protocol met the security threshold

  • duration_ms – Total execution time in milliseconds

  • channel_stats – Optional channel statistics dict

qkdpy.utils.instrumentation.record_ml_training(model_name: str, protocol: str, input_dim: int, training_samples: int, training_time_ms: float, final_loss: float | None = None, convergence_iterations: int | None = None) → None[source]

Emit a structured event for ML model training.

  • protocol – QKD protocol the model targets

  • input_dim – Input feature dimensionality

  • training_samples – Number of training samples used

  • training_time_ms – Training duration in milliseconds

  • final_loss – Final training loss value

  • convergence_iterations – Iterations to convergence

qkdpy.utils.instrumentation.record_qber_diagnostic(protocol: str, qber: float, threshold: float, key_size: int, distance_km: float | None = None) → None[source]

Emit a structured QBER diagnostic event.

Parameters:

  • protocol – Protocol name

  • qber – Measured QBER

  • threshold – Security threshold

  • key_size – Sifted key size

  • distance_km – Simulated channel distance in km

Structured logging configuration for QKDpy.

This module provides enterprise-grade structured logging using structlog, with support for JSON output, audit trails, and SIEM integration.

structlog is configured exactly once at module import time so that the processor chain is deterministic regardless of import order. Individual QKDLogger instances created by get_logger(name) are lightweight wrappers around structlog.get_logger(name) — they do NOT reconfigure structlog globally.

qkdpy.utils.logging_config.add_timestamp(logger: Any, method_name: str, event_dict: MutableMapping[str, Any]) → MutableMapping[str, Any][source]

Add ISO-8601 timestamp to log events.

qkdpy.utils.logging_config.add_qkd_context(logger: Any, method_name: str, event_dict: MutableMapping[str, Any]) → MutableMapping[str, Any][source]

Add QKD-specific context to log events.

qkdpy.utils.logging_config.redact_sensitive_data(logger: Any, method_name: str, event_dict: MutableMapping[str, Any]) → MutableMapping[str, Any][source]

Redact sensitive data from log events.

Uses an explicit denylist of full key names — substring matching (e.g., if 'key' in key.lower()) caused false positives on legitimate public fields like key_rate and qber.

class qkdpy.utils.logging_config.QKDLogger(name: str)[source]

Structured logger wrapper for QKDpy.

Each instance is a lightweight wrapper around a stdlib+structlog logger obtained via structlog.get_logger(name). The global structlog configuration is set at module-import time and never mutated by individual instances.

Input validation utilities for QKDpy.

This module provides decorators and utilities for validating function inputs, ensuring type safety, and catching invalid parameters early.

qkdpy.utils.validation.validate_range(param_name: str, min_value: float | None = None, max_value: float | None = None, *, min_inclusive: bool = True, max_inclusive: bool = True) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate numeric parameter is within range.

Parameters:

  • param_name – Name of parameter to validate

  • min_value – Minimum allowed value (None for no minimum)

  • max_value – Maximum allowed value (None for no maximum)

  • min_inclusive – Whether minimum is inclusive

  • max_inclusive – Whether maximum is inclusive

Returns:

Decorator function

qkdpy.utils.validation.validate_type(param_name: str, expected_types: type | tuple[type, ...], *, allow_none: bool = False) → Callable[[Callable[[P], R]], Callable[[P], R]][source]

Decorator to validate parameter type.

Parameters:

  • param_name – Name of parameter to validate

  • expected_types – Expected type or tuple of types

  • allow_none – Whether None is allowed

Returns:

Decorator function

Decorator to validate parameter is positive.

Parameters:

  • param_name – Name of parameter to validate

  • allow_zero – Whether zero is allowed

Decorator function

param_name – Name of parameter to validate

Decorator to validate sequence parameter is not empty.

param_name – Name of parameter to validate

Decorator function

Parameters:

qkdpy.utils.validation.validate_qber(qber: float) → None[source]

Parameters:

qber – QBER value to validate

Raises:

RangeError – If QBER is out of valid range

qkdpy.utils.validation.validate_unitary(matrix: ndarray, atol: float = 1e-10) → None[source]

Validate matrix is unitary.

Parameters:

  • matrix – Matrix to validate

  • atol – Absolute tolerance for comparison

Raises:

ParameterError – If matrix is not unitary

qkdpy.utils.validation.validate_normalized_state(state: ndarray, atol: float = 1e-10) → None[source]

Validate quantum state is normalized.

Parameters:

  • state – State vector to validate

  • atol – Absolute tolerance for comparison

Raises:

ParameterError – If state is not normalized

qkdpy.utils.validation.validate_density_matrix(rho: ndarray, atol: float = 1e-10) → None[source]

Validate density matrix properties.

Parameters:

  • rho – Density matrix to validate

  • atol – Absolute tolerance for comparison

ParameterError – If matrix is not a valid density matrix
